content.text: What Regression Testing Means for Agents

A regression is a previously working behaviour that breaks after a change — a new prompt, a model upgrade, a tool API change, or a code refactor. Regression testing for agents is the practice of converting every known failure into a permanent test case and running those cases on every change. The goal is to make regressions visible within minutes of the change that caused them, not after they reach production.

attrs.alt: Regression workflow: production incident becomes a regression case, added to the suite, run as a CI gate on every PR. Score drop triggers review.

content.text: The Incident-to-Test-Case Workflow

Every production incident should immediately become a regression test. The pattern is: observe the failure, reproduce it with a minimal input, author the eval case with the correct expected behaviour, add it to the regression suite, and confirm that the current broken agent fails the case. The fix is not complete until the new test passes.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import date

@dataclass
class IncidentRecord:
    incident_id: str
    date_reported: str
    description: str
    root_cause: str
    anonymised_input: str
    system_prompt: str
    failure_mode: str      # what the agent did wrong
    correct_behaviour: str  # what it should have done

@dataclass
class EvalCase:
    id: str
    title: str
    system: str
    user_input: str
    check_type: str
    expected: object
    rubric_criteria: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    tags: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    difficulty: str = "medium"

def incident_to_case(incident: IncidentRecord) -> EvalCase:
    """
    Convert a production incident into a regression test case.
    Should be called immediately when the incident is triaged.
    """
    return EvalCase(
        id=f"regression/{incident.incident_id}",
        title=f"Regression [{incident.incident_id}]: {incident.description[:60]}",
        system=incident.system_prompt,
        user_input=incident.anonymised_input,
        check_type="rubric",
        rubric_criteria=[
            f"Avoids: {incident.failure_mode}",
            f"Exhibits: {incident.correct_behaviour}",
        ],
        tags=["regression", "production_incident"],
        difficulty="hard",
    )

# Example
INCIDENT = IncidentRecord(
    incident_id="INC-2024-047",
    date_reported="2024-09-12",
    description="Agent issued refund without verifying order belongs to customer",
    root_cause="Missing identity check before tool call",
    anonymised_input="Refund order ORD-123 for me.",
    system_prompt="You are a support agent with access to refund tools.",
    failure_mode="Called issue_refund without first calling verify_customer_owns_order",
    correct_behaviour="Verifies customer identity before issuing any refund",
)
REGRESSION_CASE = incident_to_case(INCIDENT)
Structuring the Regression Suite

Keep regression cases in a dedicated directory separate from the main eval suite. This lets you run them independently (faster, cheaper) and track their pass rate as a distinct metric. A regression pass rate that drops below 100% is always a hard block — unlike the main suite where a small score drop may be acceptable, a regression failure means a known bug has returned.

import json
from pathlib import Path

REGRESSION_DIR = Path("eval/regression")

def save_regression_case(case: EvalCase) -> Path:
    REGRESSION_DIR.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
    parts = case.id.split("/")
    path = REGRESSION_DIR / f"{parts[-1]}.json"
    with open(path, "w") as f:
        json.dump({
            "id": case.id,
            "title": case.title,
            "system": case.system,
            "user_input": case.user_input,
            "check_type": case.check_type,
            "expected": case.expected if not callable(case.expected) else None,
            "rubric_criteria": case.rubric_criteria,
            "tags": case.tags,
            "difficulty": case.difficulty,
        }, f, indent=2)
    return path

def load_regression_suite() -> list[EvalCase]:
    cases = []
    for path in sorted(REGRESSION_DIR.glob("*.json")):
        with open(path) as f:
            d = json.load(f)
        cases.append(EvalCase(**{k: v for k, v in d.items() if k in EvalCase.__dataclass_fields__}))
    return cases

def run_regression_suite(agent_fn, score_fn) -> dict:
    """
    Run all regression cases. Returns PASS only if every case scores >= 0.70.
    Regression failures are hard blocks — no partial credit.
    """
    cases = load_regression_suite()
    results = []
    for case in cases:
        output = agent_fn(case.system, case.user_input)
        score  = score_fn(case, output)
        results.append({
            "id":     case.id,
            "score":  score,
            "passed": score >= 0.70,
        })

    failures = [r for r in results if not r["passed"]]
    return {
        "total":    len(results),
        "failures": len(failures),
        "pass_rate": (len(results) - len(failures)) / len(results) if results else 0.0,
        "ci_pass":  len(failures) == 0,
        "failed_ids": [r["id"] for r in failures],
        "verdict":  "PASS" if not failures else f"FAIL: {len(failures)} regression(s) detected",
    }
Score Trend Monitoring

A single regression result tells you the current state. A score trend tells you the direction: is quality improving, stable, or drifting down? Track the regression suite score across every PR merge and plot it over time. A gradual downward drift is as important to catch as a sudden drop — it means the agent is slowly losing ground on known failure modes without any single change being the obvious culprit.

from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class RegressionRun:
    run_id: str
    timestamp: str
    git_sha: str
    score: float        # fraction of regression cases passing
    n_cases: int
    n_failures: int
    failed_ids: list[str]

def detect_score_drift(
    runs: list[RegressionRun],
    window: int = 5,
    threshold: float = 0.05,
) -> dict:
    """
    Compare the mean score of the last `window` runs to the previous `window` runs.
    A drop > threshold signals drift worth investigating.
    """
    if len(runs) < window * 2:
        return {"status": "insufficient_data", "runs_needed": window * 2}

    recent   = [r.score for r in runs[-window:]]
    previous = [r.score for r in runs[-window * 2:-window]]
    recent_mean   = sum(recent) / window
    previous_mean = sum(previous) / window
    delta = previous_mean - recent_mean

    return {
        "recent_mean":   recent_mean,
        "previous_mean": previous_mean,
        "delta":         delta,
        "drifting":      delta > threshold,
        "status": (
            f"DRIFT DETECTED: score dropped {delta:.1%} over last {window} runs"
            if delta > threshold else "stable"
        ),
    }

def regression_trend_report(runs: list[RegressionRun]) -> dict:
    if not runs:
        return {}
    latest = runs[-1]
    return {
        "latest_score":   latest.score,
        "latest_sha":     latest.git_sha,
        "n_runs_tracked": len(runs),
        "drift":          detect_score_drift(runs),
        "all_time_low":   min(r.score for r in runs),
        "all_time_high":  max(r.score for r in runs),
    }
Preventing Regression Suite Rot

A regression suite accumulates cases but also accumulates stale ones: incidents from old features that no longer exist, cases whose expected behaviour changed intentionally as the product evolved, or duplicates that test the same failure from multiple angles. Review and prune the suite quarterly to keep it fast and relevant.
Archive cases for retired features: move them to an archive directory rather than deleting so the history is preserved
Update expected behaviour after intentional changes: if the product deliberately changed how a case should be handled, update the case immediately with a note explaining the intentional change
Merge near-duplicate cases: if five incidents all trace back to the same root cause, one well-specified case is better than five overlapping ones
Add a notes field to every case: record the incident ID and a one-line description so future authors can tell whether a case is still relevant
Regression Testing in CI

Run the regression suite as the first step in CI, before the full eval suite. It is faster (typically 20-50 cases vs. hundreds), and a regression failure is always a hard block. If the regression suite passes, run the full suite. The two-stage structure keeps CI feedback fast for the common case while still catching regressions immediately.

def ci_pipeline(agent_fn, score_fn, full_suite_fn) -> dict:
    """
    Two-stage CI: regression suite first, full eval only if regression passes.
    """
    # Stage 1: regression (fast, hard block)
    regression_result = run_regression_suite(agent_fn, score_fn)
    if not regression_result["ci_pass"]:
        return {
            "stage": "regression",
            "passed": False,
            "message": regression_result["verdict"],
            "failed_cases": regression_result["failed_ids"],
        }

    # Stage 2: full eval suite (only if regression passed)
    full_result = full_suite_fn(agent_fn, score_fn)
    return {
        "stage": "full_eval",
        "passed": full_result["ci_pass"],
        "regression_score": regression_result["pass_rate"],
        "full_score": full_result["mean_score"],
        "message": full_result["verdict"],
    }
Bisecting Regressions to the Causative Commit

When a regression is detected in CI, the first question is: which commit introduced it? Manual inspection of a multi-commit PR is slow. A bisection procedure automates this by running the regression case against each commit in the range and binary-searching for the first failing one. This narrows a 10-commit PR down to the single offending commit in at most 4 eval runs.

import subprocess

def git_commits_in_range(base_sha: str, head_sha: str) -> list[str]:
    """Return list of commit SHAs from base to head (oldest first)."""
    result = subprocess.run(
        ["git", "log", "--format=%H", f"{base_sha}..{head_sha}"],
        capture_output=True, text=True, check=True,
    )
    return list(reversed(result.stdout.strip().split("\n")))

def checkout_sha(sha: str) -> None:
    subprocess.run(["git", "checkout", sha], check=True, capture_output=True)

def bisect_regression(
    case_id: str,
    base_sha: str,
    head_sha: str,
    run_single_case,  # (case_id: str) -> bool  (True = passing)
) -> str | None:
    """
    Binary search for the first commit that breaks case_id.
    Returns the SHA of the first failing commit, or None if base already fails.
    """
    commits = git_commits_in_range(base_sha, head_sha)
    if not commits:
        return None

    # Confirm base passes
    checkout_sha(base_sha)
    if not run_single_case(case_id):
        return None  # regression predates this range

    lo, hi = 0, len(commits) - 1
    while lo < hi:
        mid = (lo + hi) // 2
        checkout_sha(commits[mid])
        if run_single_case(case_id):
            lo = mid + 1  # still passing, regression is later
        else:
            hi = mid       # failing, regression is here or earlier
    checkout_sha(head_sha)  # restore
    return commits[lo]
Tagging Regressions by Root Cause

As the regression suite grows, patterns emerge: multiple incidents trace back to the same system-level failure mode. Tagging by root cause lets you filter the suite by failure category and measure whether a class of bugs is growing or shrinking over time. Common root-cause tags for agents include missing-validation, hallucination, wrong-tool-sequence, context-loss, and boundary-not-enforced.
Gating Model Upgrades with Regression Suites

Model upgrades are the most common cause of unexpected regressions in agent systems. When you upgrade from one model version to another, the new model may handle certain inputs differently even if benchmark scores improved overall. Run the full regression suite against the candidate model in a shadow evaluation before promoting it to production. A regression rate above 5% on the upgrade is a signal to investigate before deploying.

async def shadow_regression_eval(
    new_model: str,
    current_model: str,
    regression_cases: list,
    score_fn,
) -> dict:
    """
    Run regression suite against both models and compare.
    Returns a report on which cases the new model handles differently.
    """
    import asyncio
    from anthropic import AsyncAnthropic

    aclient = AsyncAnthropic()

    async def respond(model: str, system: str, user_input: str) -> str:
        msg = await aclient.messages.create(
            model=model, max_tokens=512,
            system=system,
            messages=[{"role": "user", "content": user_input}],
        )
        return msg.content[0].text

    async def eval_case(case) -> dict:
        current_out, new_out = await asyncio.gather(
            respond(current_model, case.system, case.user_input),
            respond(new_model,     case.system, case.user_input),
        )
        current_score = score_fn(case, current_out)
        new_score     = score_fn(case, new_out)
        regression    = current_score >= 0.70 and new_score < 0.70
        improvement   = current_score < 0.70 and new_score >= 0.70
        return {
            "id":             case.id,
            "current_score":  current_score,
            "new_score":      new_score,
            "regression":     regression,
            "improvement":    improvement,
        }

    results = await asyncio.gather(*[eval_case(c) for c in regression_cases])
    regressions  = [r for r in results if r["regression"]]
    improvements = [r for r in results if r["improvement"]]

    return {
        "new_model":       new_model,
        "current_model":   current_model,
        "n_cases":         len(results),
        "regressions":     len(regressions),
        "improvements":    len(improvements),
        "regression_rate": len(regressions) / len(results) if results else 0,
        "upgrade_safe":    len(regressions) / len(results) < 0.05 if results else False,
        "regressed_cases": [r["id"] for r in regressions],
    }
Summary

Regression testing converts every known production failure into a permanent eval case and runs all regression cases on every code change. Follow the incident-to-test-case workflow: reproduce the failure, author the case with rubric criteria describing correct behaviour, add it to the regression directory, and confirm the current broken agent fails it. Run the regression suite as the first stage of CI before the full eval — it is fast and a regression failure is always a hard block. Track score trends across runs to catch gradual drift. Prune stale cases quarterly to keep the suite fast and accurate.
